preamble.summary: An elderly psalmist pleads for God's continued protection and deliverance in old age. Enemies are circling, interpreting his frailty as evidence that God has abandoned him. The psalm is a sustained cry of trust from someone who has known God since birth and now refuses to believe the final chapter will be one of abandonment. It draws heavily on Psalm 31 and Psalm 22, weaving earlier language into a new plea shaped by the particular vulnerability of aging.
preamble.remarkable: This is one of the few psalms that explicitly addresses the experience of growing old. The psalmist does not pretend that age has not diminished him — he acknowledges that his strength is failing and his enemies see opportunity. But the theological argument is powerful: the God who sustained him from the womb (verse 6), who taught him from youth (verse 17), cannot logically abandon him now. The psalm turns aging itself into a theological argument. If God's faithfulness is real, it must extend to the end. The repeated phrase 'do not cast me off' (al-tashlikeni) carries the raw urgency of someone who has watched contemporaries die alone and fears the same fate.
preamble.friction: Psalm 71 has no superscription in the Hebrew text, which is unusual in Book II of the Psalter. The LXX attributes it to David and adds a note about the sons of Jonadab and the first captives. Its heavy borrowing from Psalm 31 and Psalm 22 has led some scholars to view it as a mosaic psalm — a composition assembled from existing psalmic language. This does not diminish its theological force; it may amplify it, as the psalmist deliberately reaches for the tradition's deepest language of trust in his hour of need.
preamble.connections: Verses 1-3 closely parallel Psalm 31:2-4 (in Hebrew versification). The phrase 'from my mother's womb' (mi-beten immi) in verse 6 echoes Psalm 22:10-11, connecting this psalm to the larger tradition of lifelong divine care. The commitment to declare God's righteousness 'all day long' (kol hayyom) in verse 24 connects to the Psalter's broader theology of perpetual praise. The psalm's placement after Psalm 70 (a brief cry for help) creates a natural expansion — the short plea becomes a sustained meditation.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּךָ֣ יְ֭הוָה חָסִ֑יתִי אַל־אֵב֥וֹשָׁה לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: In thee, O LORD, do I put my trust: let me never be put to confusion.
verses.rendering:
In You, O LORD, I have taken refuge —
let me never be put to shame.
translator_notes: The near-verbatim overlap with Psalm 31:2 is intentional. The psalmist is quoting established liturgical language — reaching for words that have already been tested and proven in Israel's worship.

key_terms.semantic_range: to seek refuge, to take shelter, to trust for protection
key_terms.note: From chasah, a verb that appears frequently in the Psalms to describe the act of fleeing to God as a protective shelter. The image is concrete — a person running to a fortress or hiding in the shadow of a rock. It implies both vulnerability and active choice.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּצִדְקָתְךָ֣ תַּצִּילֵ֣נִי וּתְפַלְּטֵ֑נִי הַטֵּ֥ה אֵלַ֥י אׇ֝זְנְךָ֗ וְהוֹשִׁיעֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Deliver me in thy righteousness, and cause me to escape: incline thine ear unto me, and save me.
verses.rendering:
In Your righteousness deliver me and rescue me;
turn Your ear toward me and save me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist grounds his plea not in his own merit but in God's tsidqah ('righteousness'). This is a consistent pattern in the Psalms: God's righteousness is not merely a moral attribute but an active, saving force — God acts rightly by delivering those who trust Him. The three imperatives — tatssileni ('deliver me'), tefalleteni ('rescue me'), hoshieni ('save me') — pile up with increasing urgency, while hatteh elai ozneka ('turn Your ear toward me') personalizes the appeal.
translator_notes: The verb palat ('to rescue, to cause to escape') implies being pulled out of a tight, dangerous space — the image is of extraction from a trap or a collapsing structure.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, justice, vindication, saving acts, right order
key_terms.note: In the Psalms, God's tsidqah is not abstract moral perfection but active intervention on behalf of those who are in the right. To pray 'in Your righteousness deliver me' is to say: Your own character requires You to act.

verses.text_hebrew: הֱיֵ֤ה לִ֨י ׀ לְצ֣וּר מָ֭עוֹן לָב֣וֹא תָמִ֑יד צִוִּ֥יתָ לְ֝הוֹשִׁיעֵ֗נִי כִּֽי־סַלְעִ֥י וּמְצוּדָתִ֗י אָֽתָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Be thou my strong habitation, whereunto I may continually resort: thou hast given commandment to save me; for thou art my rock and my fortress.
verses.rendering:
Be for me a rock of refuge,
a stronghold to enter continually.
You have commanded my deliverance,
for You are my rock and my fortress.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist asks God to become a tsur maon ('rock of refuge') — a fortified dwelling place carved into stone. The phrase lavo tamid ('to enter continually') transforms the metaphor from a one-time rescue to a permanent dwelling arrangement: the psalmist wants ongoing, habitual access to God's protection. The declaration tsivvita lehoshieni ('You have commanded my deliverance') is remarkable — it treats salvation as a standing order that God has already issued, not a favor yet to be requested.
translator_notes: Psalm 31:3 reads tsur maoz ('rock of strength') while this verse has tsur maon ('rock of refuge/habitation'). The one-letter difference (zayin vs. nun) may reflect textual variation, but the Masoretic text preserves maon, emphasizing dwelling rather than mere strength.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ‍ֽלֹהַ֗י פַּ֭לְּטֵנִי מִיַּ֣ד רָשָׁ֑ע מִ֝כַּ֗ף מְעַוֵּ֥ל וְחוֹמֵֽץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Deliver me, O my God, out of the hand of the wicked, out of the hand of the unrighteous and cruel man.
verses.rendering:
My God, rescue me from the hand of the wicked,
from the grasp of the unjust and ruthless.
translator_notes: The word chomets ('ruthless, oppressive') literally means 'one who is sour, leavened' — the image is of someone whose character has fermented into cruelty. The three terms — rasha ('wicked'), me-avvel ('unjust'), and chomets ('ruthless') — paint a portrait of the psalmist's enemies as morally corrupt at every level.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אַתָּ֣ה תִקְוָתִ֑י אֲדֹנָ֥י יְ֝הוִ֗ה מִבְטַחִ֥י מִנְּעוּרָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou art my hope, O Lord GOD: thou art my trust from my youth.
verses.rendering:
For You are my hope, Lord GOD,
my trust since my youth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist now begins the autobiographical thread that makes this psalm distinctive. Tiqvati ('my hope') and mivtachi ('my trust') are not recent developments — they stretch back to ne'urai ('my youth'). The theological logic is cumulative: a lifetime of trust creates a claim. If God has been faithful for decades, the psalmist argues, abandonment now would contradict God's own track record.
translator_notes: The pairing of Adonai YHVH ('Lord GOD') is relatively rare in the Psalms and signals particular solemnity. The psalmist addresses God by His covenant name combined with the title of sovereignty.

verses.text_hebrew: עָ֘לֶ֤יךָ ׀ נִסְמַ֣כְתִּי מִבֶּ֗טֶן מִמְּעֵ֣י אִ֭מִּי אַתָּ֣ה גוֹזִ֑י בְּ֝ךָ֗ תְהִלָּתִ֥י תָמִֽיד׃
verses.text_kjv: By thee have I been holden up from the womb: thou art he that took me out of my mother's bowels: my praise shall be continually of thee.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist pushes the timeline of divine care back before conscious memory — mi-beten ('from the womb'). The rare word gozi (from gazah, 'to cut, to sever') likely refers to the cutting of the umbilical cord: God was the midwife who brought the psalmist into the world. This is intensely personal theology — God is not a distant sovereign but an intimate presence from the first moment of life. The consequence is tehillati tamid ('my praise is continual') — lifelong care produces lifelong worship.
translator_notes: The verb gazah is rare and debated. Some read it as 'my portion' (from a different root), but the context of birth imagery (beten, me'ei immi) strongly supports the meaning 'the one who cut me free' — referring to the severing of the umbilical cord at birth. This reading appears in the LXX (skepastes mou, 'my protector') by interpretive paraphrase.

verses.text_hebrew: כְּ֭מוֹפֵת הָיִ֣יתִי לְרַבִּ֑ים וְאַתָּ֖ה מַחֲסִי־עֹֽז׃
verses.text_kjv: I am as a wonder unto many; but thou art my strong refuge.
verses.rendering:
I have become a sign to many,
but You are my strong refuge.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word mofet ('sign, wonder, portent') is ambiguous and charged. It can mean a positive wonder — a miraculous sign — or a negative one — an object lesson, a cautionary example. In this context, the psalmist's suffering has made him a spectacle. People look at him and draw conclusions, likely negative ones: his affliction must mean God has rejected him. But the psalmist counters with ve-attah machasi oz ('but You are my strong refuge') — the adversative 'but' carries the entire theological weight of the verse.
translator_notes: The word mofet appears in Exodus for the plagues (signs and wonders) and in Deuteronomy 28:46 for the curses that will become 'a sign and a wonder.' The psalmist's situation has become publicly visible and is being interpreted by observers — the question is whether they are reading it correctly.

verses.text_hebrew: יִמָּ֣לֵא פִ֭י תְּהִלָּתֶ֑ךָ כׇּל־הַ֝יּ֗וֹם תִּפְאַרְתֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let my mouth be filled with thy praise and with thy honour all the day.
verses.rendering:
Let my mouth be filled with Your praise,
all day long with Your splendor.
translator_notes: The parallelism pairs tehillateka ('Your praise') with tif'artteka ('Your splendor, glory, beauty'). Praise here is not merely thanksgiving but declaration of God's magnificent character. The phrase kol hayyom ('all day long') echoes verse 24 and creates a frame of perpetual worship.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּשְׁלִיכֵ֥נִי לְעֵ֣ת זִקְנָ֑ה כִּכְל֥וֹת כֹּ֝חִ֗י אַֽל־תַּעַזְבֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Cast me not off in the time of old age; forsake me not when my strength faileth.
verses.rendering:
Do not cast me off in the time of old age;
when my strength is spent, do not abandon me.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the emotional center of the psalm. The verb tashlikeni ('cast me off, throw me away') is violent — it describes discarding something no longer useful, like a broken tool or a worn garment. The phrase le-et ziqnah ('in the time of old age') names what the psalmist fears most: not death, but abandonment before death. The parallel line — kikhlot kochi ('when my strength is finished') — acknowledges physical decline without pretense. The psalmist does not deny that he is weakening; he argues that weakness is not grounds for divine rejection.
translator_notes: This verse has no parallel in Psalm 31, confirming that Psalm 71 is not merely a copy but an original composition shaped by the experience of aging. The plea against abandonment in old age is theologically distinctive in the Psalter.

key_terms.semantic_range: old age, elderhood, the condition of being aged
key_terms.note: From zaqen ('to be old, to grow old'). In a culture that honored elders (Leviticus 19:32), the fear of being cast off in old age represents a profound social and theological crisis — it implies that the covenant of care has been broken.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אָמְר֣וּ א֭וֹיְבַי לִ֑י וְשֹׁמְרֵ֥י נַ֝פְשִׁ֗י נוֹעֲצ֥וּ יַחְדָּֽו׃
verses.text_kjv: For mine enemies speak against me; and they that lay wait for my soul take counsel together,
verses.rendering:
For my enemies speak against me,
and those who watch for my life conspire together,
translator_notes: The phrase shomrei nafshi ('those who watch for my life') describes predators watching for the moment of vulnerability. They are not merely hostile; they are strategic, waiting for the right moment to strike — and old age provides it.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֭אמֹר אֱלֹהִ֣ים עֲזָב֑וֹ רִ֝דְפ֗וּ וְתִפְשׂ֥וּהוּ כִּי־אֵ֥ין מַצִּֽיל׃
verses.text_kjv: Saying, God hath forsaken him: persecute and take him; for there is none to deliver him.
verses.rendering:
saying, 'God has abandoned him.
Pursue and seize him,
for there is no one to rescue him.'
translator_notes: The enemies' statement mirrors the taunt against the sufferer in Psalm 22:9 and Psalm 3:3 — the recurring claim that God has withdrawn. The psalmist must contend not only with his enemies but with their theology.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ‍ֽלֹהִ֗ים אַל־תִּרְחַ֥ק מִמֶּ֑נִּי אֱ֝לֹהַ֗י לְעֶזְרָ֥תִי חֽוּשָׁה׃
verses.text_kjv: O God, be not far from me: O my God, make haste for my help.
verses.rendering:
O God, do not be far from me;
my God, hurry to help me!
translator_notes: The verb chushah ('hurry, hasten') conveys desperation — the situation is urgent and delay could be fatal. This verse echoes Psalm 22:12 and 22:20, reinforcing the literary connections between these psalms.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵבֹ֣שׁוּ יִ֭כְלוּ שׂוֹטְנֵ֣י נַפְשִׁ֑י יַֽעֲט֥וּ חֶ֝רְפָּ֗ה וּכְלִמָּ֥ה מְבַקְשֵׁ֥י רָעָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Let them be confounded and consumed that are adversaries to my soul; let them be covered with reproach and dishonour that seek my hurt.
translator_notes: The verb ya'atu ('let them be wrapped, clothed') uses clothing imagery for shame — disgrace becomes a garment that covers them completely. The psalmist asks that the enemies' own weapon (public shame) be turned back upon them.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֗י תָּ֭מִיד אֲיַחֵ֑ל וְ֝הוֹסַפְתִּ֗י עַל־כׇּל־תְּהִלָּתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: But I will hope continually, and will yet praise thee more and more.
verses.rendering:
But I — I will hope continually,
and I will add still more to all Your praise.
verses.expanded_rendering: The emphatic va-ani ('but I') marks the decisive turn in the psalm. The enemies have spoken their verdict (verses 10-11); now the psalmist speaks his. The word tamid ('continually, always') is the same word used in verse 6 for continual praise — it creates a thread of persistence that runs through the entire psalm. The phrase vehosafti al kol tehillateka ('and I will add upon all Your praise') is remarkable: the psalmist promises not just to continue praising but to increase his praise. Suffering does not diminish worship; it intensifies it.
translator_notes: The verb yachal ('to hope, to wait') in the Psalms always implies patient endurance under pressure — not passive optimism but active, stubborn trust maintained against contrary evidence.

verses.text_hebrew: פִּ֗י יְסַפֵּ֣ר צִ֭דְקָתְךָ כׇּל־הַיּ֣וֹם תְּשׁוּעָתֶ֑ךָ כִּ֥י לֹ֝֗א יָדַ֥עְתִּי סְפֹרֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: My mouth shall shew forth thy righteousness and thy salvation all the day; for I know not the numbers thereof.
verses.rendering:
My mouth will declare Your righteousness,
all day long Your salvation,
for I cannot count them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist commits to declaring God's tsidqah ('righteousness') and teshuah ('salvation') — the same paired concepts that drive the psalm. The final phrase ki lo yadati seforot ('for I do not know the numbers') is a confession of being overwhelmed: God's righteous acts are so numerous that the psalmist cannot tally them. This is not innumeracy but awe — the same response as Psalm 40:6, where God's wonders are 'too many to declare.'
translator_notes: The word seforot ('numbers, reckonings') is rare and may also carry the sense of 'literary compositions' — the psalmist may be saying not only that he cannot count God's acts but that he lacks the literary skill to do them justice.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֭בוֹא בִּגְבֻר֣וֹת אֲדֹנָ֣י יְהוִ֑ה אַזְכִּ֖יר צִדְקָתְךָ֣ לְבַדֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I will go in the strength of the Lord GOD: I will make mention of thy righteousness, even of thine only.
verses.rendering:
I will come with the mighty deeds of the Lord GOD;
I will declare Your righteousness — Yours alone.
translator_notes: The phrase gevurot Adonai YHVH ('mighty deeds of the Lord GOD') refers to God's powerful acts in history — not abstract power but demonstrated intervention. The emphasis levaddeka ('Yours alone') is exclusive: the psalmist credits no one else, no other god, no human agency.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ‍ֽלֹהִ֗ים לִמַּדְתַּ֥נִי מִנְּעוּרָ֑י וְעַד־הֵ֝֗נָּה אַגִּ֥יד נִפְלְאוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: O God, thou hast taught me from my youth: and hitherto have I declared thy wondrous works.
verses.rendering:
O God, You have taught me from my youth,
and until now I have declared Your wonders.
translator_notes: The word nifl'ot ('wonders') refers to God's extraordinary, incomprehensible acts — the same word used for the Exodus miracles. The psalmist's personal experience of God is framed in the same language as Israel's national experience.

verses.text_hebrew: וְגַ֤ם עַד־זִקְנָ֨ה ׀ וְשֵׂיבָ֗ה אֱלֹהִ֥ים אַֽל־תַּעַזְבֵ֑נִי עַד־אַגִּ֥יד זְ֝רוֹעֲךָ֗ לְד֣וֹר לְכׇל־יָבֹ֥וא גְּבוּרָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Now also when I am old and greyheaded, O God, forsake me not; until I have shewed thy strength unto this generation, and thy power to every one that is to come.
verses.rendering:
And even to old age and gray hair,
O God, do not abandon me,
until I declare Your arm to a generation,
Your power to all who are yet to come.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is the psalm's theological climax. The psalmist names his condition plainly — ziqnah ve-sevah ('old age and gray hair') — and then makes a stunning argument: do not abandon me yet because I still have work to do. The 'arm' of God (zero'akha) is His power in action, His intervention in history. The psalmist positions himself as a witness whose testimony is not yet complete — there is a generation (dor) that still needs to hear, and people yet to come (kol yavo) who must learn what God has done. Abandonment now would not only harm the psalmist; it would silence a witness.
translator_notes: The argument that God should preserve the psalmist for the sake of future testimony is a recurring motif in the Psalms (cf. Psalm 6:6, 30:10, 88:11-13). The dead cannot praise God — therefore God has a vested interest in keeping His worshipers alive.

key_terms.semantic_range: arm, strength, power, might — especially God's intervening power in history
key_terms.note: The 'arm of the LORD' is one of the Hebrew Bible's most important metaphors for divine action. It appears in the Exodus narrative (Exodus 6:6), in the prophets (Isaiah 53:1), and throughout the Psalms. It means power applied — not potential but enacted.

verses.text_hebrew: וְצִדְקָתְךָ֥ אֱלֹהִ֗ים עַד־מָ֫ר֥וֹם אֲשֶׁר־עָשִׂ֥יתָ גְדֹל֑וֹת אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים מִ֣י כָמֽוֹךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy righteousness also, O God, is very high, who hast done great things: O God, who is like unto thee!
verses.expanded_rendering: The exclamation mi kamokha ('who is like You?') echoes the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:11), one of the oldest texts in the Hebrew Bible. The psalmist's personal experience of God opens onto the cosmic: God's righteousness extends ad marom ('to the heights') — it fills the heavens. The rhetorical question expects no answer because none exists. There is no comparison.
translator_notes: The phrase mi kamokha is a declaration of incomparability that runs through Israel's worship from the Exodus to the Psalter. It is not philosophical monotheism but experiential — no other power has done what this God has done.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֤ר הִרְאִיתַ֨נוּ ׀ צָר֥וֹת רַבּ֗וֹת וְרָ֫ע֥וֹת תָּ֭שׁוּב תְּחַיֵּ֑נוּ וּמִתְּהֹמ֥וֹת הָ֝אָ֗רֶץ תָּשׁ֥וּב תַּעֲלֵֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou, which hast shewed me great and sore troubles, shalt quicken me again, and shalt bring me up again from the depths of the earth.
verses.rendering:
You who have made us see many and bitter troubles
will revive us again,
and from the depths of the earth
You will bring us up again.
verses.expanded_rendering: The Hebrew shifts between singular and plural pronouns (some manuscripts read 'me' rather than 'us'), but the Masoretic text has hir'itanu ('You have shown us') — the psalmist's personal suffering is placed within the community's experience. The verb techayyenu ('You will revive us') carries the force of resurrection language — bringing back to life what was as good as dead. The 'depths of the earth' (tehomot ha-arets) evokes Sheol, the realm of the dead. The psalmist is saying: even if I descend to the very threshold of death, You will pull me back.
translator_notes: The Qere/Ketiv variation between singular and plural pronouns in this verse reflects textual uncertainty about whether the psalm is purely individual or has been adapted for communal use. The Masoretic Qere reads plural ('us'), suggesting liturgical adaptation.

verses.text_hebrew: תֶּ֣רֶב גְּדֻלָּתִ֑י וְ֝תִסֹּ֗ב תְּנַחֲמֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt increase my greatness, and comfort me on every side.
verses.rendering:
You will increase my honor
and turn to comfort me.
translator_notes: The word gedullatai ('my greatness, my honor, my dignity') is significant for an elderly person whose enemies have declared him abandoned. God will not merely rescue him — God will restore his standing. The verb tissov ('You will turn around') suggests God turning back toward the psalmist with comfort after a season of apparent distance.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־אֲנִ֤י ׀ אוֹדְךָ֣ בִכְלִי־נֶ֭בֶל אֲמִתְּךָ֗ אֱלֹהָ֑י אֲזַמְּרָ֥ה לְ֝ךָ֗ בְכִנּ֥וֹר קְד֣וֹשׁ יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: I will also praise thee with the psaltery, even thy truth, O my God: unto thee will I sing with the harp, O thou Holy One of Israel.
verses.rendering:
I also will praise You with the harp
for Your faithfulness, my God.
I will make music to You with the lyre,
O Holy One of Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist pledges instrumental worship — the nevel (harp or lute) and kinnor (lyre) — the same instruments David played. The phrase amitteka ('Your faithfulness, Your truth') is what the psalmist celebrates: not that suffering has ended but that God's character has proven reliable. The title Qedosh Yisrael ('Holy One of Israel') is primarily an Isaianic title (Isaiah 1:4, 5:19, etc.) and is rare in the Psalms, appearing only here and in Psalm 78:41 and 89:19. Its presence connects the personal psalm to prophetic theology.
translator_notes: The nevel was a larger stringed instrument, possibly a type of angular harp, while the kinnor was the smaller lyre associated with David (1 Samuel 16:23). Together they represent full musical worship — the psalmist's response to deliverance will be public and artistic.

key_terms.semantic_range: the set-apart one, the sacred one; God in His distinctive separateness from all creation
key_terms.note: This title emphasizes God's otherness — His radical distinction from everything created. In Isaiah, it often appears in contexts of both judgment and redemption. Its use here connects the psalmist's personal experience to Israel's prophetic tradition.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּרַנֵּ֣נָּה שְׂ֭פָתַי כִּ֣י אֲזַמְּרָה־לָּ֑ךְ וְ֝נַפְשִׁ֗י אֲשֶׁ֣ר פָּדִֽיתָ׃
verses.text_kjv: My lips shall greatly rejoice when I sing unto thee; and my soul, which thou hast redeemed.
verses.rendering:
My lips will shout for joy when I make music to You —
my very life, which You have redeemed.
translator_notes: The verb padita ('You have redeemed') uses the language of payment and liberation — padah means to buy back, to ransom, to liberate by paying a price. The psalmist's life has been purchased back from the grip of death and enemies.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־לְ֭שׁוֹנִי כׇּל־הַיּ֗וֹם תֶּהְגֶּ֥ה צִדְקָתֶ֑ךָ כִּי־בֹ֥שׁוּ כִֽי־חָ֝פְר֗וּ מְבַקְשֵׁ֥י רָעָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: My tongue also shall talk of thy righteousness all the day long: for they are brought to shame, for they are confounded, that seek my hurt.
verses.rendering:
My tongue also, all day long,
will murmur of Your righteousness,
for those who sought my ruin
have been shamed and disgraced.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends where it began — with God's tsidqah ('righteousness'). The verb tehgeh ('will murmur, will meditate aloud') is the same verb used in Psalm 1:2 for meditating on Torah day and night. The psalmist's tongue will work continuously, like a person who cannot stop thinking about something. The final line provides the resolution: the enemies who said 'God has abandoned him' have themselves been shamed. Their theology was wrong. God did not abandon His servant, and their public disgrace proves it.
translator_notes: The pairing of boshu ('they are ashamed') and chafru ('they are disgraced') is a standard Hebrew word pair for total public humiliation. The psalmist who feared being put to shame (verse 1) now sees that shame transferred to his enemies — the reversal is complete.
